title: Structured Cabling & Media
summary: Copper and fiber media types and their distance/speed limits, TIA/EIA-568 termination standards and pinouts, the hardware that terminates and patches cable, fiber optics and transceivers, coax, and the testing, certification, tools, and labeling standards a Diplomatic Technology Officer needs to install and document an embassy's physical plant.
content:
Structured cabling is the standardized, hierarchical physical wiring of a building, and the foundation is balanced copper twisted pair. The pairs are twisted to cancel electromagnetic interference (EMI) and crosstalk; tighter twists support higher frequencies. • Cat5e is rated to 100 MHz and supports 1000BASE-T (1 Gbps) to 100 m — the practical legacy minimum. • Cat6 is rated to 250 MHz: it runs 1 Gbps to 100 m, but 10GBASE-T (10 Gbps) only to roughly 37–55 m because of alien crosstalk. • Cat6a ('augmented') is rated to 500 MHz and carries 10 Gbps over the full 100 m — the modern enterprise/embassy default. • Cat7/Cat7a (600 MHz–1 GHz) is a shielded standard popular in Europe but uses non-RJ45 (GG45/TERA) connectors and was never ratified by TIA. • Cat8 (2000 MHz) supports 25/40 Gbps but only to 30 m, so it is a short data-center top-of-rack/switch-to-switch medium, not a horizontal run.

Shielding and conductor construction matter for noisy or secure environments. • UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) relies only on the twists; it is cheap, flexible, and dominant in offices. • Shielded variants add metal: F/UTP (foil around all four pairs), S/FTP (braid screen plus per-pair foil — the best EMI rejection), and the older STP. Shielding helps in high-EMI areas (near motors, generators, transmitters) and reduces emanations, but it must be properly bonded and grounded at the ends or it can act as an antenna and make things worse. • Solid-core conductors are for permanent horizontal/in-wall runs (lower attenuation, terminated on punch-down blocks); stranded conductors are flexible and used for patch cords (terminated in RJ45 plugs) because they survive repeated flexing.

The 100-meter channel rule governs every horizontal copper run: a maximum of 90 m of solid permanent-link cable between the patch panel and the wall outlet, plus up to 10 m total of stranded patch cords at both ends (telecom room and work area) = 100 m channel. Exceed it and you get attenuation, timing, and bit-error problems; longer runs require fiber or an intermediate switch.

Termination follows the ANSI/TIA-568 standard, which defines two eight-position pin assignments for the four pairs in an RJ45. • T568A and T568B differ only in swapping the green and orange pairs. • Pins: T568B (the more common in U.S./commercial work) is white-orange, orange, white-green, blue, white-blue, green, white-brown, brown on pins 1–8. T568A is the U.S. federal/government and residential default and is backward-compatible with USOC voice. The rule: pick one standard and use it consistently across the site. • A straight-through cable uses the same standard on both ends and connects unlike devices (PC↔switch). • A crossover cable uses T568A on one end and T568B on the other, swapping transmit/receive to connect like devices (switch↔switch, PC↔PC). • Auto-MDIX (Medium Dependent Interface Crossover) on modern switch ports detects and electronically swaps the pairs, so crossover cables are largely obsolete — but the exam still tests the pinout logic.

Termination hardware ties the system together. • A patch panel is the central termination point in the telecom room: horizontal solid cable is punched down on the back, and short patch cords on the front connect ports to switches. • A keystone jack is the modular wall-outlet termination at the work-area end. • Punch-down blocks seat conductors into insulation-displacement contacts (IDC): the older 66 block was for analog voice/PBX (high crosstalk), while the 110 block (and modern 110-style panels/jacks) supports data up to Cat6a and is the current standard. A punch-down tool seats and trims the wire in one stroke. • RJ45 (8P8C) is the 8-conductor data connector; RJ11 is the smaller 6-position connector for analog telephone lines — they are not interchangeable.

Fiber optics carries light instead of electricity and is the backbone and inter-building medium. • Single-mode fiber (SMF, ~9 µm core, yellow jacket) uses a laser, carries one ray, and reaches very long distances (km to tens of km) — used for campus backbones and WAN. • Multimode fiber (MMF, 50/62.5 µm core, aqua/orange jacket) uses cheaper LEDs/VCSELs over shorter distances; OM ratings step up reach: OM1/OM2 (legacy, orange), OM3 (aqua, 300 m at 10G), OM4 (aqua, ~400 m at 10G / ~150 m at 40-100G), OM5 (lime green, wideband for SWDM). • Connectors: LC (small form-factor, the modern default), SC (square push-pull), ST (older bayonet), and MTP/MPO (multi-fiber ribbon connector for 40/100G breakout). • Transceivers are hot-swappable optics: SFP (1G), SFP+ (10G), SFP28 (25G), QSFP+ (40G), and QSFP28 (100G) — matched to the switch port and fiber type. • Fibers run simplex (one strand) or, normally, duplex (a transmit + a receive strand). Fiber vs. copper trade-offs: fiber gives far greater distance and bandwidth, total immunity to EMI/RFI (critical near radio/satellite gear), no crosstalk, and a major security advantage — it emits no electromagnetic signal to capture and is extremely hard to tap without detectable light-loss; copper is cheaper, simpler to terminate in the field, and can carry power (PoE), which fiber cannot.

Coaxial cable persists for specific roles. • RG-6 (thicker, lower loss) is used for CATV, satellite, and broadband/cable-modem drops; RG-59 (thinner, higher loss) is legacy for short CCTV/video runs. • Connectors: the threaded F-type for RG-6 broadband/TV, and the bayonet BNC for RG-59 video, test gear, and legacy 10BASE2.

A cable is only trustworthy if it is tested and certified. • Continuity confirms each conductor is unbroken end to end. • A wiremap detects opens, shorts, reversed pairs, transposed/crossed pairs, and split pairs (the classic mis-termination that passes continuity but fails performance). • Length/TDR (Time-Domain Reflectometry) measures cable length and locates the distance to a fault or break by timing a reflected pulse; an OTDR does the optical equivalent for fiber. • Certification testers measure attenuation/insertion loss (signal weakening over distance), return loss, and crosstalk — NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk), the noise a pair induces in an adjacent pair measured at the near end, plus FEXT/ACR — comparing results against the Cat5e/6/6a frequency limits to issue a pass/fail certificate. Tools of the trade: a crimper (attaches RJ45 plugs), a punch-down tool (terminates IDC blocks/jacks), a cable tester (continuity/wiremap), a toner and probe (tone generator + inductive probe to trace and identify a specific cable in a bundle), and a fusion splicer (permanently welds two fiber ends with very low loss).

Labeling and documentation are mandatory, not optional, and are governed by ANSI/TIA-606 (administration standard). It defines a consistent labeling scheme and color coding for cables, patch panels, outlets, and pathways, plus the records (cable IDs, port maps, test results) that let a future technician trace any circuit. For embassies this is also a security and accountability control: every drop is identified, mapped to a controlled-access space, and auditable.
keyPoints: Copper categories: Cat5e (100 MHz, 1G/100m), Cat6 (250 MHz, 1G/100m but 10G only ~37–55m), Cat6a (500 MHz, 10G/100m), Cat8 (2000 MHz, 25/40G to 30m for data-center).
UTP relies on twists; F/UTP and S/FTP add foil/braid shielding for high-EMI/secure areas but must be bonded and grounded at the ends.
Solid-core cable = permanent in-wall runs on punch-downs; stranded = flexible patch cords with RJ45 plugs.
100m channel rule = 90m solid permanent link + up to 10m of stranded patch cords at the two ends.
TIA-568 T568A and T568B differ only by swapping the green and orange pairs; T568A is the U.S. federal/government default, T568B is common commercial.
Straight-through (same standard both ends) connects unlike devices; crossover (568A/568B mixed) connects like devices; Auto-MDIX makes crossovers obsolete.
Patch panel + keystone jack terminate solid cable; 110 block supports data (Cat6a), the legacy 66 block was analog voice; RJ45 (8P8C) data vs. RJ11 voice.
Single-mode fiber (9µm, laser, long-haul) vs. multimode (50/62.5µm, LED/VCSEL, OM3/OM4/OM5 short-reach); connectors LC/SC/ST/MTP; transceivers SFP(1G)/SFP+(10G)/QSFP(40-100G).
Fiber advantages: distance, bandwidth, total EMI immunity, no crosstalk, and security (no emanations, very hard to tap undetected); copper is cheaper and can carry PoE.
Coax: RG-6 (F-type) for broadband/CATV/satellite; RG-59 (BNC) for legacy video/CCTV.
Testing/certification: continuity, wiremap (opens/shorts/reversed/split pairs), length/TDR (OTDR for fiber), attenuation/insertion loss, and NEXT crosstalk against category limits.
TIA-606 governs labeling, color coding, and documentation of cables, panels, and outlets — both a maintenance and a security/audit control.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum distance and speed of Cat6a?
flashcards.back: 10 Gbps (10GBASE-T) over the full 100 meters; rated to 500 MHz.

flashcards.front: Why does Cat6 not reliably do 10G to 100m like Cat6a does?
flashcards.back: At 10 Gbps Cat6 is limited to roughly 37–55 m because of alien crosstalk; Cat6a's tighter design and 500 MHz rating fix this for 100 m.

flashcards.front: State the 100-meter channel rule.
flashcards.back: Up to 90 m of solid permanent-link cable plus up to 10 m total of stranded patch cords at both ends = 100 m channel maximum.

flashcards.front: Solid-core vs. stranded copper — where is each used?
flashcards.back: Solid for permanent in-wall horizontal runs (punch-down terminated, lower attenuation); stranded for flexible patch cords (RJ45 plugs, survive flexing).

flashcards.front: How do T568A and T568B differ?
flashcards.back: They swap the green and orange pairs; everything else (blue and brown pairs) is identical.

flashcards.front: Which TIA-568 standard is the U.S. federal/government default?
flashcards.back: T568A (also residential/USOC-compatible); T568B is the common U.S. commercial choice.

flashcards.front: When do you need a crossover cable, and what makes it obsolete?
flashcards.back: To connect like devices (switch-switch, PC-PC) by using T568A on one end and T568B on the other; Auto-MDIX makes it obsolete by auto-swapping the pairs.

flashcards.front: 66 block vs. 110 block?
flashcards.back: 66 block is legacy analog voice/PBX (high crosstalk); 110 block supports data up to Cat6a and is the current standard.

flashcards.front: RJ45 vs. RJ11?
flashcards.back: RJ45 (8P8C) is the 8-conductor data connector; RJ11 is the 6-position connector for analog phone lines — not interchangeable.

flashcards.front: Single-mode vs. multimode fiber — core, light source, and reach?
flashcards.back: Single-mode ~9µm core, laser, very long distance; multimode 50/62.5µm core, LED/VCSEL, shorter distance (OM3 300m, OM4 ~400m at 10G).

flashcards.front: Match transceivers to speed: SFP, SFP+, QSFP28.
flashcards.back: SFP = 1 Gbps, SFP+ = 10 Gbps, QSFP28 = 100 Gbps (QSFP+ = 40 Gbps).

flashcards.front: Why is fiber preferred for security and high-EMI sites?
flashcards.back: It carries light, so it emits no electromagnetic signal to intercept, is extremely hard to tap without detectable light loss, and is fully immune to EMI/RFI and crosstalk.

flashcards.front: RG-6 vs. RG-59 coax and their connectors?
flashcards.back: RG-6 (F-type) = broadband/CATV/satellite, lower loss; RG-59 (BNC) = legacy short video/CCTV runs, higher loss.

flashcards.front: What is NEXT?
flashcards.back: Near-End Crosstalk — the unwanted signal a transmitting pair induces in an adjacent pair, measured at the same (near) end of the cable.

flashcards.front: What does a wiremap test detect that simple continuity does not?
flashcards.back: Opens, shorts, reversed pairs, transposed/crossed pairs, and split pairs (which pass continuity but fail performance).

flashcards.front: What does TDR/OTDR measure?
flashcards.back: Cable length and the distance to a fault/break, by timing a reflected pulse (TDR for copper, OTDR for fiber).

flashcards.front: What does a toner and probe do?
flashcards.back: A tone generator injects an audible signal on a cable, and the inductive probe traces and identifies that specific cable in a bundle or at the far end.

flashcards.front: Which standard governs cabling labeling and documentation?
flashcards.back: ANSI/TIA-606 — the administration standard for labeling, color coding, and records of cables, panels, outlets, and pathways.

title: Site & Facilities Infrastructure
summary: Equipment racks and cable management, the MDF/IDF hierarchy and demarcation point, power infrastructure (PDU/UPS/generator/ATS), grounding and bonding, server-room environmental controls and fire suppression, KVM and out-of-band access, physical security and emanation awareness for controlled spaces, the WAN demarc/CSU-DSU/ONT, and the resilience model that keeps an embassy's network running.
content:
The physical home of an embassy's IT is the equipment rack, and its dimensions are standardized. • The 19-inch rack is the universal width (mounting rails 19 in apart). • Height and equipment are measured in rack units (U), where 1U = 1.75 inches; a full rack is typically 42U. • A two-post rack (relay rack) supports lighter gear (patch panels, switches) by its front; a four-post rack (or enclosed cabinet) supports the front and rear of heavy equipment (servers, UPS) and is the data-center norm. • Cable management — horizontal and vertical organizers, lacing bars, and proper bend-radius routing — is not cosmetic: it preserves airflow, prevents kinking/attenuation, and makes the plant maintainable and auditable.

The wiring hierarchy connects those racks. • The MDF (Main Distribution Frame), also called the Main Equipment Room or, in TIA terms, the Equipment Room, is the building's central termination and where the carrier's service and core switching/routing live. • IDFs (Intermediate Distribution Frames), or Telecommunications Rooms, are floor/wing wiring closets that aggregate the horizontal cabling from work-area outlets and connect back to the MDF via backbone (riser) cabling, almost always fiber. • The demarcation point (demarc) is the legal and physical boundary where the service provider's responsibility ends and the customer's wiring begins — typically a block or jack where the WAN circuit enters the building. Everything past the demarc is the post's responsibility.

Power infrastructure keeps everything alive and is layered for redundancy. • A PDU (Power Distribution Unit) distributes power to rack equipment; intelligent/switched PDUs add per-outlet metering and remote on/off. • A UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) bridges the gap during an outage and conditions power. Types matter: a standby/offline UPS switches to battery only on failure (cheapest, for desktops); a line-interactive UPS adds an autotransformer (AVR) to correct minor sags/surges without going to battery (good for small server/network gear); and an online double-conversion UPS continuously runs the load off the inverter (AC→DC→AC), giving zero transfer time and the cleanest power — the standard for critical server rooms. • UPS sizing is done in VA and watts: total the connected load, apply the power factor, and add headroom; runtime is set so a generator can start or systems can shut down gracefully. • For extended outages a generator provides long-term power, and an ATS (Automatic Transfer Switch) senses the utility failure and switches the load from mains to generator (and back) automatically. • True resilience uses redundant power: dual power supplies in each device fed from two independent PDUs/UPS/utility feeds (A/B power), so any single failure is survivable.

Grounding and bonding protect both people and equipment, and the standard is ANSI/TIA-607. It defines the telecommunications bonding and grounding system: a TMGB (main grounding busbar) in the entrance facility, TGBs (grounding busbars) in each telecom room, and bonding conductors tying racks, cable trays, shielded cabling, and panels to a common ground. Proper bonding prevents dangerous potential differences (ground loops), drains static, and gives surge/lightning energy a safe path. Surge protective devices (SPDs) and lightning arrestors at the building entrance and on outdoor/antenna runs divert transients to ground before they reach electronics.

Server rooms need tight environmental control. • Temperature: ASHRAE recommends roughly 18–27 °C (64–81 °F) at the equipment intake; humidity is kept in a band (about 40–60% RH) — too dry invites static discharge, too humid invites condensation and corrosion. • A CRAC (Computer Room Air Conditioner) or CRAH/precision HVAC unit provides dedicated cooling. • Hot-aisle/cold-aisle layout faces equipment intakes toward cold aisles (fed by perforated tiles) and exhausts into hot aisles returning to the CRAC, with containment to stop the air mixing. • A raised floor can serve as a cold-air plenum and a cable pathway. • Fire suppression is chosen to protect equipment: clean-agent gaseous systems (FM-200, Novec 1230, inert gas like Inergen) smother the fire without leaving residue or damaging electronics and are the data-center preference; pre-action (dry-pipe) water sprinklers add a second trigger so a single false alarm cannot release water; standard wet-pipe sprinklers protect life but destroy equipment. Smoke/VESDA early detection complements suppression.

Managing the gear remotely needs the right access paths. • A KVM (Keyboard, Video, Mouse) switch lets one console operate many servers; KVM-over-IP extends that across the network. • Out-of-band (OOB) / console access is the critical management discriminator: a dedicated path (serial console server, dedicated management NIC, or cellular/separate link) that reaches a device even when the production network or the device's main interface is down — essential for an embassy where a remote technician must recover a router without flying to post.

Physical security of IT spaces is a core Diplomatic Technology Officer duty. • Access control layers locks, badge/PIN/biometric readers, mantraps, CCTV, and visitor logs around server rooms and wiring closets. • Sensitive processing happens in controlled access areas (CAA) / controlled-access spaces — physically protected rooms with stricter entry, escort, and inspection rules. • Emanation security (the discipline historically associated with TEMPEST) recognizes that electronic equipment radiates signals that could be intercepted; mitigations include shielded equipment/cabling, shielded enclosures, red/black separation (segregating classified 'red' signal wiring from unclassified 'black'), and physical inspection zones. • Equipment and media destruction closes the lifecycle: drives and media are sanitized (cryptographic erase, degaussing) or physically destroyed (shredding, disintegration, incineration) per policy so no residual classified data leaves the controlled space.

The post connects to the wider world through the WAN demarcation hardware. • For a digital leased circuit (T1/E1), a CSU/DSU (Channel Service Unit/Data Service Unit) terminates the line and adapts it to the customer router; for fiber broadband, an ONT (Optical Network Terminal) converts the provider's optical signal to Ethernet/electrical. • These sit at or just inside the demarc and hand off to the customer edge router. • Resilience at the WAN edge follows the PACE model — Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency — a layered communications plan from State/DoD doctrine: a primary circuit (e.g., fiber MPLS), an alternate (a second carrier or path), a contingency (satellite/VSAT), and an emergency last resort (e.g., HF radio or cellular). Combined with redundant circuits from diverse carriers entering via diverse physical paths, redundant power, and OOB management, PACE ensures an embassy stays connected even when the primary infrastructure fails.
keyPoints: Racks are 19 inches wide and measured in U (1U = 1.75 in, full rack ~42U); two-post for light gear, four-post/cabinet for servers and heavy equipment.
MDF (main equipment room, carrier handoff + core) connects to IDFs (telecom rooms/closets) via fiber backbone/riser cabling.
The demarcation point is the boundary between the service provider's and the customer's responsibility for the circuit.
UPS types: standby/offline (cheapest), line-interactive (adds AVR for sags/surges), online double-conversion (continuous, zero transfer time — for critical server rooms).
UPS is sized in VA/watts (load × power factor + headroom); a generator + ATS handles long outages by auto-switching mains to generator.
Redundant (A/B) power = dual supplies fed by independent PDUs/UPS/utility feeds so any single failure is survivable.
TIA-607 defines telecom bonding/grounding (TMGB, TGB, bonding conductors); SPDs/lightning arrestors divert surges to ground.
Server-room targets: ~18–27 °C and ~40–60% RH; use CRAC cooling and hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment, optionally a raised-floor plenum.
Clean-agent gas suppression (FM-200, Novec 1230, inert gas) protects electronics without residue; pre-action water adds a double trigger; wet-pipe damages gear.
Out-of-band/console access is a dedicated management path that works even when the production network or device interface is down.
Physical security: access control, controlled access areas (CAA), emanation/TEMPEST awareness with red/black separation, and media sanitization/destruction (degauss, shred, crypto-erase).
WAN handoff: CSU/DSU terminates a T1/E1 leased line, an ONT converts fiber to Ethernet; resilience follows PACE (Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency).

flashcards.front: What is a rack unit (U) and a 19-inch rack?
flashcards.back: 1U = 1.75 inches of vertical mounting space; the 19-inch rack is the standard equipment width (rails 19 in apart), commonly 42U tall.

flashcards.front: Two-post vs. four-post rack?
flashcards.back: Two-post (relay rack) front-mounts light gear like patch panels/switches; four-post (or cabinet) supports front and rear for heavy servers and UPS — the data-center standard.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between an MDF and an IDF?
flashcards.back: The MDF is the building's central/main equipment room with the carrier handoff and core gear; IDFs are floor/wing telecom closets that aggregate horizontal cabling back to the MDF via backbone fiber.

flashcards.front: What is the demarcation point?
flashcards.back: The physical/legal boundary where the service provider's responsibility ends and the customer's internal wiring begins.

flashcards.front: Name the three UPS topologies in order of protection.
flashcards.back: Standby/offline (battery only on failure), line-interactive (adds AVR for sags/surges), online double-conversion (continuous AC-DC-AC, zero transfer time).

flashcards.front: Which UPS type is standard for a critical server room and why?
flashcards.back: Online double-conversion — the load always runs off the inverter, so there is zero transfer time and the cleanest, fully isolated power.

flashcards.front: What does an ATS do?
flashcards.back: An Automatic Transfer Switch senses a utility failure and automatically switches the load from mains to the generator (and back when utility returns).

flashcards.front: What is A/B (redundant) power?
flashcards.back: Each device has dual power supplies fed from two independent PDU/UPS/utility feeds, so any single power-path failure is survivable.

flashcards.front: Which standard covers telecom grounding and bonding?
flashcards.back: ANSI/TIA-607 — defines the TMGB, TGBs, and bonding conductors that tie racks, trays, shields, and panels to a common ground.

flashcards.front: What temperature and humidity range is recommended for a server room?
flashcards.back: Roughly 18–27 °C (64–81 °F) at equipment intake and about 40–60% relative humidity (ASHRAE).

flashcards.front: How does hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment work?
flashcards.back: Equipment intakes face cold aisles fed by cool air; exhaust blows into hot aisles that return to the CRAC, with containment preventing the airstreams from mixing.

flashcards.front: Why use clean-agent fire suppression in a data center?
flashcards.back: Gaseous agents (FM-200, Novec 1230, inert gas) extinguish fire without water or residue, so they protect electronics; wet-pipe sprinklers would destroy the equipment.

flashcards.front: What is out-of-band (OOB) management and why does it matter?
flashcards.back: A dedicated console/management path (serial console server, mgmt NIC, cellular) that reaches a device even when the production network or its main interface is down — vital for remote recovery at an embassy.

flashcards.front: What is a controlled access area (CAA)?
flashcards.back: A physically protected space with stricter entry, escort, and inspection rules where sensitive/classified processing occurs.

flashcards.front: What does TEMPEST / emanation security address?
flashcards.back: The risk that electronics radiate interceptable signals; mitigations include shielding, shielded enclosures, and red/black separation of classified vs. unclassified wiring.

flashcards.front: Name two approved methods of media destruction/sanitization.
flashcards.back: Degaussing or physical destruction (shredding/disintegration/incineration); cryptographic erase for self-encrypting drives.

flashcards.front: CSU/DSU vs. ONT — what does each terminate?
flashcards.back: A CSU/DSU terminates a digital leased line (T1/E1) and adapts it to the router; an ONT converts a provider's fiber optical signal to Ethernet.

flashcards.front: What does the PACE model stand for and what is it for?
flashcards.back: Primary, Alternate, Contingency, Emergency — a layered communications/resilience plan ensuring connectivity survives the loss of the primary path (e.g., fiber, second carrier, satellite, HF radio).
